title: Kairos: A Native World Model Stack for Physical AI
why: Kairos proposes treating world models as operational infrastructure for robotics, with a hybrid attention scheme the authors formally claim bounds long-horizon error accumulation, directly relevant to anyone building real-time physical agents rather than offline video generators.
notes: Kairos structures the problem around three requirements: learning from heterogeneous experience via a Cross-Embodiment Data Curriculum (open-world video, human behavioral data, robot interactions organized into a progressive training pathway); maintaining state via a unified understanding/generation/prediction architecture; and running within real deployment constraints on both server and consumer-grade hardware.

The core architectural contribution is Hybrid Linear Temporal Attention, which composes three mechanisms: sliding-window attention for local dynamics, dilated sliding windows for mid-range dependencies, and gated linear attention for persistent global memory, trading quadratic complexity for linear scaling across time.

The authors provide formal theoretical bounds claiming that their temporal factorization strictly limits error accumulation and mathematically guarantees state propagation over extended horizons; these are self-reported proofs in a June 2026 arXiv preprint, not independently verified.

Deployment-Aware System Co-Design is listed as a first-class design pillar targeting low-latency rollout generation on consumer hardware, signaling intent to ship rather than benchmark only, though no concrete latency numbers or hardware specifics appear in the abstract.

Benchmarked on embodied world-model, long-horizon, and action-policy tasks with claimed top-tier performance and a favorable efficiency-capability trade-off; the abstract does not report specific scores or name the competing baselines.
summary: Kairos is a world model stack for Physical AI introduced in a June 2026 arXiv preprint, organized around three design pillars. First, a Cross-Embodiment Data Curriculum structures training progressively across open-world video, human behavioral data, and robot interaction data to build transferable world knowledge. Second, a Native Unified Architecture employs Hybrid Linear Temporal Attention, composing sliding-window, dilated sliding-window, and gated linear attention to handle local dynamics, mid-range context, and persistent global memory within a single pass; the authors provide theoretical bounds claiming this temporal factorization strictly limits error accumulation over long horizons. Third, Deployment-Aware System Co-Design targets low-latency inference on consumer-grade as well as server hardware, framing the stack as production infrastructure rather than a research artifact. Evaluations on embodied world-model, long-horizon, and action-policy benchmarks reportedly show top-level performance with strong efficiency-capability trade-offs, but the abstract omits specific scores, baselines, and hardware configurations.
novelty: Native world model stack for Physical AI with unified architecture and deployment-aware system co-design
audience: AI researchers and engineers working on physical intelligence

title: GameCraft-Bench: Can Agents Build Playable Games End-to-End in a Real Game Engine?
why: GameCraft-Bench reveals a concrete, measurable ceiling for frontier coding agents on multi-artifact integration tasks, where producing correct code is necessary but not sufficient for a working system.
notes: The benchmark comprises 140 tasks in the Godot engine across 15 game families; evaluation is runtime-grounded, using replayed gameplay demonstrations and rubric-guided multimodal judging, not static code analysis or unit tests.

The authors define three evaluation desiderata: Engine Grounding (execution inside a real engine), Artifact Completeness (all components, scripts, scenes, and assets present), and Interactive Verification (assessed through observable player-game interaction).
The strongest frontier coding agent scores 41.46%; most agents fall below 40%, suggesting end-to-end game generation is a hard open problem rather than a near-solved one.

Failure mode analysis is specific: agents often produce recognizable game mechanics but fail on content completeness, functional visual feedback, and coherent presentation, pointing to integration and multi-step planning gaps rather than pure syntax errors.

The benchmark explicitly targets a gap in existing coding evaluations: game generation requires scripts, scenes, assets, rendering, and runtime interactions to jointly produce coherent gameplay, a multi-artifact dependency structure absent from function-level or file-level benchmarks.
summary: GameCraft-Bench is a benchmark of 140 Godot engine tasks across 15 game families that evaluates coding agents on end-to-end game generation from natural-language specifications. The authors argue the setting demands three properties absent from standard benchmarks: Engine Grounding (execution inside a real game engine), Artifact Completeness (all game components present and consistent), and Interactive Verification (assessed via actual gameplay rather than static analysis). Evaluation uses replayed game demonstrations scored by rubric-guided multimodal judges. Frontier coding agents score poorly: the best achieves 41.46% and most fall below 40%. Failure analysis identifies specific shortfalls in content volume, visual feedback, and presentation coherence rather than in basic mechanical implementation, suggesting that compositional integration across a heterogeneous artifact set is the limiting factor.
novelty: end-to-end game generation using coding agents in a real game engine
audience: AI researchers and game developers

title: Aligning Quantum Operators with Large Language Models
why: If the approach generalizes, embedding quantum operators directly into LLM latent space could make language-driven quantum circuit synthesis a practical compiler primitive rather than a research curiosity.
notes: The core mechanism maps unitary matrices into an LLM's latent space so the model can reason jointly over quantum operators and natural language; the abstract does not specify whether this is a learned projection head, cross-attention, or another adapter architecture.

The testbed is Clifford+T circuit synthesis over a Pauli rotation gate set, a standard but narrow benchmark in quantum compilation; results are described only as 'competitive with state-of-the-art' with no numeric figures given in the available text.

Language-conditioned synthesis is the operationally novel claim: gate constraints not seen during training can be specified in natural language at inference time, suggesting a path to prompt-driven compiler passes.

The authors report consistent scaling with training data and 'no signs of saturation,' implying the task may benefit from continued compute investment, though saturation curves and dataset sizes are not disclosed in the abstract.
Stated downstream ambitions are quantum compilation and algorithm discovery; the paper is an arXiv preprint (cs.AI / quant-ph, June 2026) and has not yet been peer-reviewed.
summary: This arXiv preprint proposes embedding quantum unitary operators into the latent space of a large language model, enabling a single model to ingest both quantum-circuit and natural-language inputs. The authors instantiate the idea on Clifford+T circuit synthesis over a Pauli rotation gate set, reporting results they describe as competitive with state-of-the-art methods and consistent scaling with training data without saturation, though no numeric benchmark figures are provided in the abstract. The most operationally interesting claim is language-conditioned synthesis: gate constraints absent from the training distribution can be communicated to the model in plain English at inference time. The paper frames this as a step toward quantum-aware foundation models capable of supporting quantum compilation pipelines and algorithm discovery. Methodological details, including the precise mechanism for projecting unitary matrices into the LLM latent space and the datasets used, are not disclosed in the available abstract text. The work is a preprint and has not undergone peer review.
novelty: quantum operators alignment with LLMs
audience: quantum computing researchers and engineers

title: Orchestra-o1: Omnimodal Agent Orchestration
why: If the benchmark results hold up under scrutiny, Orchestra-o1 is the first orchestration framework to route heterogeneous text/image/audio/video sub-tasks to specialist agents at runtime, with a purpose-built RL training method, establishing a new reference point for omnimodal agentic pipelines.
notes: Orchestra-o1 claims a 10.3% accuracy improvement over the second-best approach on the OmniGAIA benchmark; the abstract does not name the baseline systems or provide per-modality breakdowns, so independent verification of this figure requires reading the full paper.

Three architectural mechanisms are described: modality-aware task decomposition (routing sub-tasks by input type), online sub-agent specialization (agents are assigned dynamically at inference time rather than statically pre-configured), and parallel sub-task execution across those agents.

DA-GRPO (decision-aligned group relative policy optimization) is introduced as a new agentic RL training algorithm used to produce Orchestra-o1-8B; it is characterized as an extension of GRPO adapted for multi-agent, multi-modal agentic settings, though architectural details are not in the abstract.
Orchestra-o1-8B is claimed to achieve state-of-the-art performance against all existing open-source omnimodal agents; no comparison to closed-source frontier systems is stated in the abstract.

The paper frames the core gap as: prior orchestration frameworks (implicitly including frameworks like AutoGen, LangGraph, and similar) are limited to narrow modality sets and cannot generalize to scenarios where text, image, audio, and video coexist and must be jointly coordinated.
summary: Orchestra-o1 is an omnimodal agent orchestration framework that extends multi-agent LLM systems to coordinate across text, image, audio, and video inputs within a single unified pipeline. Its core design introduces three mechanisms: modality-aware task decomposition, online sub-agent specialization (dynamic agent assignment at inference time), and parallel sub-task execution. On the OmniGAIA benchmark the authors report a 10.3% accuracy gain over the second-best approach, though the abstract does not identify the baselines or provide per-modality ablations. Alongside the framework, the paper introduces DA-GRPO (decision-aligned group relative policy optimization), an agentic reinforcement learning method used to train the open-weight Orchestra-o1-8B model, which the authors claim achieves state-of-the-art among open-source omnimodal agents. The work positions itself as addressing a structural gap in existing orchestration frameworks, which the authors characterize as modality-restricted and unable to generalize to heterogeneous real-world tasks.
novelty: omnimodal agent orchestration framework
audience: AI researchers and engineers working on multi-agent systems

title: Memory is Reconstructed, Not Retrieved: Graph Memory for LLM Agents
why: The static retrieve-then-reason pipeline is a known weak point in production LLM agents, and this paper proposes a concrete architectural replacement with benchmark results suggesting the problem is tractable today.
notes: The paper's core critique is that existing memory-augmented agents use a rigid 'static retrieve-then-reason' pipeline that cannot revise what it retrieves based on evidence discovered mid-inference, a structural limitation rather than a tuning problem.

MRAgent represents memory as a Cue-Tag-Content graph: fine-grained query cues are connected to stored memory content via associative tags that act as semantic bridges, making the retrieval structure navigable by reasoning.

The 'active reconstruction mechanism' embeds LLM reasoning directly inside the retrieval loop, iteratively exploring and pruning graph traversal paths as evidence accumulates; the authors claim this avoids the combinatorial explosion of unconstrained graph expansion.

On the LoCoMo and LongMemEval benchmarks (both target long-interaction-history reasoning), MRAgent is reported to achieve up to 23% improvement over 'strong baselines'; specific baseline identities and per-benchmark breakdowns are not disclosed in the abstract.

The authors claim simultaneous reduction in token usage and runtime cost alongside accuracy gains, but no absolute numbers, ablation studies, or methodology details are available from the abstract alone.
summary: MRAgent is a proposed framework for long-horizon memory reasoning in LLM agents that replaces the conventional static retrieve-then-reason pipeline with an active reconstruction mechanism operating over a structured graph. Memory is encoded as a Cue-Tag-Content graph in which associative tags serve as semantic bridges between fine-grained query cues and stored content, enabling structured traversal rather than flat vector lookup. Instead of retrieving a fixed context block and passing it downstream, MRAgent integrates LLM reasoning into the retrieval loop itself, iteratively expanding and pruning graph paths as intermediate evidence accumulates during inference. The authors argue this design prevents both the brittleness of one-shot retrieval and the combinatorial explosion of unconstrained graph search. Evaluated on LoCoMo and LongMemEval, the system is reported to yield up to 23% gains over unspecified strong baselines while also reducing token consumption and runtime; detailed methodology, ablations, and baseline identities are not available from the abstract.
novelty: Dynamic memory access through active reconstruction
audience: AI researchers and developers working on LLM agents

title: APPO: Agentic Procedural Policy Optimization
why: Credit assignment is the core bottleneck in agentic RL training, and APPO's empirical finding that influential decision points are distributed throughout generated sequences, not concentrated at tool-call boundaries, directly challenges the design assumptions baked into most current agentic training pipelines.
notes: Pilot analysis finding: influential decision points are broadly distributed throughout generated sequences, not at tool-call boundaries or fixed-workflow checkpoints; token entropy alone does not reliably identify them.

APPO's Branching Score combines token uncertainty with policy-induced likelihood gains of subsequent continuations, specifically to filter spurious high-entropy positions that don't actually affect outcomes.
Procedure-level advantage scaling is introduced to distribute credit across branched rollouts, replacing the coarse heuristic units (tool-call boundaries, fixed workflows) used by prior methods.
Claimed result: nearly 4 percentage-point improvement over strong agentic RL baselines across 13 benchmarks, with no reported regression on tool-call efficiency or behavior interpretability.

Caveat: the paper is an arXiv preprint; benchmark identities, baseline definitions, and evaluation methodology are not detailed in the abstract, so the 4-point headline number cannot yet be independently assessed.
summary: APPO (Agentic Procedural Policy Optimization) is an agentic RL training method that reframes where branching and credit assignment occur during multi-turn LLM agent training. Existing methods assign credit at coarse interaction units such as tool-call boundaries or fixed workflows; the authors' pilot analysis finds that the decision points that actually influence downstream outcomes are spread throughout the full generated sequence, and that token entropy is an unreliable proxy for identifying them. APPO addresses this with a Branching Score that combines token uncertainty with policy-induced likelihood gains of subsequent continuations, selecting branching locations more precisely while filtering high-entropy positions that carry little causal weight. A companion mechanism, procedure-level advantage scaling, distributes advantage estimates across branched rollouts rather than collapsing credit to interaction-level signals. Across 13 benchmarks, the method is claimed to improve strong agentic RL baselines by nearly 4 points while preserving efficient tool-call behavior and interpretability. The work is a preprint; full benchmark details and baseline specifications are not available in the abstract.
novelty: fine-grained decision point branching and credit assignment in agentic RL
audience: researchers in reinforcement learning and large language model agents

title: Skip a Layer or Loop It? Learning Program-of-Layers in LLMs
why: Fixed-depth, fixed-order inference is a design choice, not a fundamental constraint: this paper demonstrates that pretrained LLM layers already encode multiple valid computation paths, and routing around or through them per-input can simultaneously cut compute and raise accuracy.
notes: PoLar (Program-of-Layers) reframes inference as a dynamic program over pretrained layer-modules: each input gets its own schedule of skips and loops, with no modification to base model weights; the authors call this regime 'training-free' in the sense that the base LLM is unchanged.

A lightweight prediction network is the only new learned component; it is trained to emit per-input execution programs specifying which layers to skip or repeat, sitting atop frozen pretrained weights.

On mathematical reasoning benchmarks, PoLar improves accuracy over both standard fixed-depth inference and prior dynamic-depth methods, and does so while executing fewer total layers on most inputs, though the abstract provides no specific accuracy deltas or layer-reduction percentages.

The paper claims that incorrect predictions from standard forward passes can be corrected by alternative PoLar programs that use fewer layers, which implies that some errors in base-model inference are artifacts of forced fixed-depth execution rather than missing knowledge.

Out-of-distribution evaluation is reported to preserve the gains, which is a meaningful robustness check, but the entire experimental scope is confined to mathematical reasoning; generalization to other task families is not established in the abstract.
summary: This paper from arXiv (2606.06574) introduces Program-of-Layers (PoLar), a framework that treats pretrained LLM layers as composable modules that can be dynamically skipped or looped on a per-input basis rather than always executed in a fixed sequential depth. The central empirical claim is that diverse valid execution programs exist within pretrained models without any weight modification, and that for most inputs these shorter or reordered programs match or exceed the accuracy of the standard forward pass. A lightweight prediction network is trained to select per-input execution programs at inference time; it is the only component that requires learning. Experiments on mathematical reasoning benchmarks show PoLar outperforms both standard inference and prior dynamic-depth baselines, often while processing fewer layers, with gains reported to persist under out-of-distribution evaluation. The authors interpret this as evidence that fixed-depth inference accesses only a narrow subset of the latent reasoning capacity encoded in pretrained weights. The abstract supplies no specific accuracy or efficiency numbers, and all evaluation is on mathematical reasoning tasks, so claims about broader applicability remain unsubstantiated by the reported evidence.
novelty: dynamic program-of-layers (PoLar) in LLMs
audience: ML researchers and engineers working with large language models

title: Measuring Epistemic Resilience of LLMs Under Misleading Medical Context
why: LLMs that score at expert level on medical licensing exams may still flip to wrong answers when a patient or clinician frames the question with plausible-sounding misinformation, meaning benchmark accuracy tells you almost nothing about robustness in the adversarial conditions of real medical use.
notes: Across 11 model configurations, mean accuracy collapsed from 71.1% on clean questions to 38.0% under focused misleading context injection, yielding an overall attack success rate of 51.5%; the models are not degrading randomly -- they are actively abandoning previously correct answers.

The most effective attack vectors are formal, rule-like fabrications: authority-framed falsehoods achieved 69.5% attack success and exception-poisoning claims achieved 64.1%, suggesting models are disproportionately susceptible to text that mimics clinical guidelines or policy language.

MedMisBench comprises 10,932 medical question items paired with 48,889 misleading context-option pairs, spanning three evaluation axes: medical reasoning, agentic capability, and patient-journey scenarios.

A 14-member international clinical panel drawn from 7 countries reviewed model failures and identified serious potential harm in 38.2% of cases, providing a clinical-stakes grounding beyond purely automated metrics.

The paper's structural argument: existing medical benchmarks measure knowledge retrieval but not preservation of correct judgment under adversarial pressure; the authors term this missing property 'epistemic resilience' and frame MedMisBench as the first systematic attempt to quantify it.
summary: This paper introduces epistemic resilience as a measurable LLM property: the ability to maintain a correct answer when misleading context is injected into an otherwise correctly answered question. To operationalize this, the authors release MedMisBench, a benchmark of 10,932 medical questions paired with 48,889 misleading context-option pairs covering medical reasoning, agentic tasks, and patient-journey scenarios. Testing 11 model configurations, they observe mean accuracy falling from 71.1% to 38.0% under focused misleading context, with an overall attack success rate of 51.5%; the most damaging injection types are authority-framed falsehoods (69.5% attack success) and exception-poisoning claims (64.1%), both of which mimic the register of formal clinical rules. A 14-member clinical panel from 7 countries reviewed sampled failures and flagged serious potential harm in 38.2% of cases. The authors' central claim is that existing medical benchmarks expose only what models have learned, not whether that knowledge is preserved under adversarial pressure, and that this gap constitutes a structural blind spot in how AI readiness for medical deployment is currently evaluated.
novelty: epistemic resilience measurement in LLMs
audience: AI researchers and developers working on medical applications

title: Anthropic 'Suspends' All Mythos and Fable Access After US Order Limiting Foreign Access
why: The US government has issued what appears to be the first emergency export-control directive against a named deployed commercial AI model, forcing Anthropic to globally pull Fable 5 and Mythos 5 over a jailbreak Anthropic publicly characterizes as trivially available in competing models — establishing a precedent with industry-wide consequences.
notes: Jailbreak justification cited by government: 'asking the model to read a specific codebase and fix any software flaws' — Anthropic says equivalent capability exists in GPT-5.5 and is used daily by security defenders; only verbal evidence provided, no written disclosure of a harmful result.

Scope is maximally broad: foreign nationals 'whether inside or outside the United States, including foreign national Anthropic employees' — forces global takedown; named Anthropic staff affected include co-founder Chris Olah, researcher Andrej Karpathy, and philosopher Amanda Askell.
AWS was separately directed to revoke access for 'all users in all regions' — enforcement reaches the infrastructure layer, not just Anthropic's own API surface.

Anthropic's core policy objection: if a narrow, non-universal jailbreak triggers mandatory recall of a model deployed to hundreds of millions, 'it would essentially halt all new model deployments for all frontier model providers.'

Dean Ball (former White House official, contributor to the 2025 AI Action Plan) interprets the order as requiring citizenship verification to access frontier Anthropic models — no statutory process, no transparency, no written technical basis given to the company.
summary: The US government issued an emergency directive requiring Anthropic to suspend access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5 for all foreign nationals, citing a claimed jailbreak technique involving prompting the model to read a codebase and identify software flaws. Anthropic is complying but publicly disputing the justification, arguing the demonstrated capability is widely available in competing models including GPT-5.5 and provides no model-specific uplift. The directive's extraterritorial scope — covering foreign nationals both inside and outside the US, including Anthropic's own employees — necessitated a full global shutdown; AWS was separately instructed to revoke access for all users in all regions. Anthropic warns that applying this standard industry-wide would effectively freeze all frontier model deployments, and frames the action as lacking the statutory transparency, written evidence, and technical grounding it believes proper export-control oversight requires. The company says it is working to restore access and believes the situation is a misunderstanding.
novelty: First known US government emergency export-control action against a specific deployed commercial AI model by name; reveals the government's claimed harm threshold (code-review/debugging capability) is dramatically lower than industry assumed; establishes that a non-universal jailbreak with no disclosed harmful result can trigger mandatory global suspension affecting hundreds of millions of users; surfaces the absence of any statutory process governing such directives.
audience: AI platform engineers and policy-watchers with production Anthropic API dependencies or interest in frontier-model governance

title: A 0-click exploit chain for the Pixel 9 Part 1: Decoding Dolby
why: Project Zero just published a fully working 0-click exploit chain against the Pixel 9—with step-by-step technical detail—demonstrating that AI-powered audio transcription silently expanded Android's attack surface to include every Dolby-licensed device.
notes: CVE-2025-54957: integer overflow in Dolby UDC's evo_malloc alignment fixup (`total_size += (8 - total_size) % total_size`) when total_size is 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF9–0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF, producing a wrap-to-small allocation while the write loop still uses the original large payload_length as its bound.

Attack surface created by AI feature: Google Messages auto-decodes RCS/SMS audio attachments (for transcription) before the user opens the message — making the Dolby UDC a 0-click entry point on most Android devices, as UDC ships as a binary blob statically linked by most OEMs.

Two derived primitives: 'buffer overrun capability' (up to 3066 attacker-controlled OOB bytes per syncframe chain) and 'leak capability' (EMDF bit reader uses attacker-supplied emdf_container_length rather than actual skip buffer fill length, enabling reads beyond initialized memory).

ASLR partial bypass requires guessing one nibble of scudo secondary chunk address (1-in-16 odds); failed attempts crash and respawn mediacodec in ~3 seconds, so average exploit success is ~24 seconds of repeated sends.

Exploit chain requires decoding multiple audio files in sequence: first corrupts scudo's `curr chunk len` metadata on the dynamic buffer to shift the heap header by 0x2000 on the next decoder reset, then uses the gained writable space to place an EMDF container that overwrites function pointers at static buffer offsets 0x8a410/0x8a438; all three CVEs patched January 5, 2026.
summary: Google Project Zero (Adam Donenfeld, Ivan Fratric, Seth Jenkins) published Part 1 of a three-part series detailing a working 0-click exploit chain against the Pixel 9. The root vulnerability, CVE-2025-54957, is an integer overflow in the Dolby Unified Decoder's custom evo heap allocator: an alignment fixup lacks an overflow check, allowing a crafted EMDF payload to produce a tiny allocation while the copy loop uses the original large length, enabling a controlled out-of-bounds write up to 3066 bytes. A second "leak capability" arises because the EMDF bit reader bounds are set from an attacker-supplied container length field rather than the actual filled skip buffer size. The 0-click delivery path was opened by Google Messages' automatic audio transcription feature, which decodes incoming RCS/SMS audio attachments before any user interaction; the Dolby UDC is a binary blob shipped to most Android OEMs and statically linked into their media stacks. The exploit chains multiple syncframe decodes and a scudo secondary-chunk metadata corruption to ultimately overwrite function pointers deep in the static buffer, achieving arbitrary code execution in the mediacodec sandbox. Part 2 covers kernel escalation via CVE-2025-36934; all vulnerabilities were fixed January 5, 2026.
novelty: End-to-end demonstration that AI transcription features (not just media playback) create 0-click decoder attack surface; novel scudo secondary-chunk metadata corruption technique to shift heap header and gain pre-header writable space; published proof that such exploits are achievable by non-nation-state researchers against modern Android with all mitigations enabled.
audience: Android/mobile security researchers, platform engineers responsible for media stack sandboxing, and anyone evaluating whether automatic AI feature rollouts introduce new attack surface.

title: Statement on the US government directive to suspend access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5
why: The US government used export control authority to force Anthropic to globally kill two frontier models within hours, based on contested jailbreak evidence — a first-of-kind precedent for national-security-driven AI access shutoffs.
notes: Export control directive received 5:21pm ET June 12; all foreign nationals (including Anthropic employees) lost access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5; Simon Willison's polling script confirmed cutoff at 6:59pm PT (~10pm ET) with 404 errors redirecting to Opus 4.8.
Government's stated jailbreak concern: prompting the model to 'read a specific codebase and fix any software flaws' — framed as a national security threat enabling vulnerability discovery.

Anthropic publicly disputes severity: validated capability is available from other models including OpenAI GPT-5.5, and is used daily by security defenders; vulnerabilities found were described as 'minor' and 'previously known'.
Government provided only verbal evidence of a 'narrow, non-universal jailbreak'; no written justification or specific national security rationale shared with Anthropic at time of publication.
Conflict of interest caveat: Anthropic's public statement is self-serving; no independent third-party assessment of the jailbreak's actual capability ceiling is cited in the article.
summary: On June 12, 2026, the US government issued an export control directive requiring Anthropic to immediately suspend all access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5 for any foreign national, globally, citing national security concerns about a jailbreak. Access was cut off approximately four hours after the directive was received. The government's concern centers on a technique involving prompting the model to read codebases and identify software flaws, which it believes could enable exploitation of vulnerabilities. Anthropic contested the severity in a public statement, arguing the demonstrated capability is widely available from other frontier models (explicitly naming GPT-5.5) and is standard practice among security defenders. The government offered only verbal evidence of the jailbreak and provided no written justification; Anthropic committed to releasing further details within 24 hours. The cutoff was independently confirmed by Simon Willison via an API polling script, which captured the exact moment of model unavailability.
novelty: First documented instance of the US government invoking export control authority to force a frontier AI lab to globally and abruptly suspend access to specific named model versions based on jailbreak claims; the speed of enforcement (directive to full cutoff in ~5 hours) and the contestation of whether code-review capability constitutes a national security threat are both novel.
audience: AI/ML engineers and platform teams building on Anthropic's API, especially those with international users or employees, and anyone tracking AI governance and export control policy.

title: As a result of a US Government directive, we are suspending access to Fable 5
why: The US government has invoked national security export controls to suspend access to Anthropic's Fable 5 (and Mythos 5) for all foreign nationals globally—likely the first such action targeting a specific civilian frontier AI model—with immediate API breakage for developers and sweeping policy precedent implications.
notes: The directive covers 'any foreign national, whether inside or outside the United States, including foreign national Anthropic employees'—an unusually broad citizenship-based access split that cleaves even the developer company's own workforce.
Immediate engineering impact: Claude Platform API calls to Fable 5 return errors; developers must actively migrate integrations; Claude products fall back to Opus 4.8 or user-selected default model.
Both Fable 5 and Mythos 5 are suspended simultaneously, suggesting the directive targets a capability tier rather than a single model artifact.
Source is truncated—the 'net effect of…' clause is cut off, so the full stated scope of restrictions is unconfirmed from this text alone; independent verification from anthropic.com is warranted.

Primary source is a tweet from @ClaudeDevs (likely official Anthropic developer channel), not an anthropic.com blog post or press release—treat as high-credibility but unverified until cross-confirmed.
summary: Anthropic's developer Twitter account (@ClaudeDevs) announced that the US government issued a national security export control directive requiring suspension of Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5 access for all foreign nationals, including those physically inside the United States and Anthropic's own foreign national employees. Across Claude products, sessions default to Opus 4.8 or a user's selected model; existing Fable 5 sessions terminate with errors and API integrations must be actively updated. If confirmed, this appears to be the first time US national security authority has been invoked to restrict a specific civilian frontier AI model as a controlled export—a qualitative step beyond existing chip export controls. The source text is truncated, leaving the full stated "net effect" clause unknown. The action sets a potential regulatory precedent treating frontier AI models analogously to advanced semiconductors or dual-use cryptographic software.
novelty: First known invocation of US national security export control authority to restrict access to a specific civilian frontier AI model; the citizenship-based restriction extends to foreign nationals inside US borders and within the AI company itself—a qualitatively new form of AI governance with no direct prior analogue in the software sector.
audience: Claude API developers who need to migrate integrations immediately, and AI policy researchers tracking the emergence of frontier-model export controls.

title: US Government directive to suspend access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5
why: The US government has, for the first time, used export control authority to force a frontier AI model offline mid-deployment over a disputed narrow jailbreak finding — a precedent that Anthropic itself warns would halt all future frontier model launches if applied consistently.
notes: Government directive arrived 5:21pm ET with no prior specific disclosure; the letter cited national security but withheld technical details — Anthropic's account of the reasoning comes from a separate report it says it obtained and reviewed independently.

The triggering jailbreak is narrow and non-universal: prompting the model to read a codebase and identify software flaws; Anthropic validated the same capability is present in GPT-5.5 and other publicly available models, meaning the finding provides no Fable-specific uplift.

Fable 5 received thousands of hours of pre-launch red-teaming with US government, UK AISI, and private third parties; no universal jailbreak was found, and Anthropic had publicly stated at launch that perfect jailbreak resistance is unachievable for any provider.

Anthropic's 30-day customer data retention policy — imposed specifically to enable jailbreak monitoring and resisted by some customers — is cited as part of its defense-in-depth strategy, underscoring that safety trade-offs were deliberate and pre-disclosed.
Primary source caveat: this is Anthropic's one-sided public statement; the government's specific technical evidence and legal basis have not been independently released or verified.
summary: The US government issued an export control directive requiring Anthropic to suspend access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5 for all foreign nationals, forcing Anthropic to disable both models for its entire global customer base to ensure compliance. The stated basis is a potential non-universal jailbreak enabling software vulnerability discovery; Anthropic reviewed what it believes is the government's supporting report and disputes both the severity and the Fable-specificity of the capability, asserting GPT-5.5 and other public models can do the same. Fable 5 had undergone extensive pre-launch government and third-party red-teaming, and Anthropic had publicly acknowledged that perfect jailbreak resistance is not achievable, adopting a defense-in-depth strategy with mandatory data retention for monitoring. Anthropic is complying with the directive while publicly challenging the standard, warning that applying this threshold across the industry would effectively block all new frontier model deployments. The company plans to release additional technical details within 24 hours. All other Anthropic models remain accessible.
novelty: First known use of US export control authority to compel suspension of a commercially deployed frontier AI model; Anthropic's public, on-record rebuttal with specific technical parity claims against GPT-5.5; explicit government–industry confrontation over what constitutes an unsafe jailbreak threshold.
audience: Frontier model developers, AI policy practitioners, and enterprise customers navigating compliance exposure for models with foreign-national users.

title: Text-Vision Co-Instructed Image Editing
why: Practical image editing still forces a choice between semantic precision (text) and spatial precision (drag/point); this paper proposes a unified conditioning mechanism that could make that tradeoff unnecessary in production editing tools.
notes: The core framing: text instructions are semantically expressive but spatially coarse; drag/point visual prompts are spatially precise but semantically ambiguous. TV-Edit is designed to exploit both simultaneously rather than choosing one.

Training data: 23K textual-visual instruction pairs derived from dynamic videos, not static images; the video source is the mechanism for generating paired before/after edits with aligned cross-modal supervision.

Architecture: TV-Edit contextualizes drag or point-based visual instructions with image-text semantics, then lifts them into 'semantic-aware control representations' that feed into pretrained editing backbones. The paper describes this as an adapter-style approach compatible with multiple backbones, not a from-scratch model.

Benchmark: TV-Edit-Bench evaluates three axes, semantic faithfulness, spatial alignment, and visual consistency, using ground-truth references and controlled textual-visual input variations; this is a deliberate attempt to make evaluation less susceptible to cherry-picking.

Caveat: results are self-reported comparisons against text-only and drag-based baselines across multiple editing backbones; no code or independent replication is referenced in the abstract, and the paper appeared on arXiv in June 2026 without attached demo links in the provided text.
summary: TV-Edit introduces a joint conditioning framework for image editing that combines natural-language instructions as semantic intent with sparse spatial signals (drag vectors or point clicks) as spatial guidance, addressing the complementary weaknesses of text-only and drag-only pipelines. To train the cross-modal instruction model, the authors build a 23K-sample paired dataset sourced from dynamic videos, which provides naturally aligned before/after editing pairs with both modalities. The framework converts visual instructions into semantic-aware control representations that are injected into pretrained editing backbones, positioning TV-Edit as a modular adapter rather than a standalone generative model. Alongside the method, the authors release TV-Edit-Bench, a benchmark that assesses semantic faithfulness, spatial alignment, and visual consistency using ground-truth references and controlled input variations to enable more reliable comparison. Experiments across multiple backbone architectures report consistent outperformance over state-of-the-art text-instruction and drag-based baselines, though all evaluation is self-reported and no independent replication or public code release is noted in the abstract at time of publication.
novelty: unified text-vision co-instruction for image editing
audience: computer vision researchers

title: Beyond the Current Observation: Evaluating Multimodal Large Language Models in Controllable Non-Markov Games
why: Existing MLLM benchmarks do not cleanly test whether a model can reconstruct and act on observations it can no longer see, so RNG-Bench fills a gap directly relevant to building closed-loop agentic systems that must maintain state across long, partially-observable episodes.
notes: RNG-Bench (Reconstructive Non-Markov Games) contains two purpose-built games: Matching Pairs, where card identities revealed at specific locations must be recalled after being hidden, and 3D Maze, where egocentric views must be integrated into a coherent spatial map; both are parameterized along three difficulty axes: grid size, visual pattern, and observation modality.
Hardest configurations demand roughly 128K-token contexts with approximately 350 image inputs per episode; the paper reports these configurations remain far from saturated by current frontier MLLMs.

A dedicated Memory Gap metric is introduced to disentangle two failure modes: forgetting earlier observations versus suboptimal action selection given correct state reconstruction; the paper's analysis concludes that forgetting dominates residual errors, not planning quality.

A head-to-head duel protocol pits two agents on identical instances to control for instance-level variance, addressing a methodological weakness of single-agent episode scoring common in prior benchmarks.

Fine-tuning Qwen3.5-9B on optimal-policy rollouts and filtered model demonstrations improves RNG-Bench scores and transfers positively to existing multimodal benchmarks without degrading general capability, suggesting the memory-reconstruction skill is learnable and not domain-locked.
summary: RNG-Bench is a new benchmark suite targeting a specific capability gap in multimodal LLMs: the ability to reconstruct observations that are no longer visible and act correctly on that reconstructed state during closed-loop, multi-step interaction. The suite comprises two controllable games, Matching Pairs and 3D Maze, each varying in grid size, visual pattern complexity, and observation modality, and evaluated under a unified harness. Two methodological contributions accompany the games: a duel protocol that pairs agents on identical instances to suppress instance variance, and a Memory Gap metric that separates forgetting failures from action-selection failures. Analysis using this metric finds that most errors in current frontier MLLMs trace to memory loss rather than to poor planning given correct recall. The hardest benchmark configurations, requiring contexts of roughly 128K tokens and 350 images per episode, are far from solved by existing models. Fine-tuning Qwen3.5-9B on optimal-policy and filtered-demonstration rollouts improves benchmark performance and transfers to held-out multimodal evaluations without general capability regression.
novelty: Evaluating multimodal large language models in controllable non-Markov games with a focus on reconstructing past observations
audience: ML researchers and engineers working on multimodal foundation models

title: LectūraAgents: A Multi-Agent Framework for Adaptive Personalized AI-Assisted Learning and Embodied Teaching
why: For engineers building agentic systems, LectūraAgents is worth skimming for its hierarchical orchestration pattern and the TASA temporal-alignment algorithm, which address a general problem: synchronizing agent-generated actions with agent-generated speech across a structured, multi-phase pipeline.
notes: Hierarchical architecture: a ProfessorAgent orchestrates specialized subordinate agents through four sequential phases (research, planning, review, embodied delivery), mirroring a professor-student delegation model.

Embodied teaching actions are concrete, parameterized operations (handwrite, highlight, underline) executed over a virtual teaching environment; this is the main differentiator from prior lecture-automation systems that output only text or slides.

The Teaching Action-Speech Alignment (TASA) algorithm uses salience-based heuristics and temporal semantic segmentation to produce action sequences coherent with both the spoken lecture and the target learner profile.

Evaluation methodology is rubric-based qualitative analysis validated by expert educators across high school, undergraduate, and graduate courses; the abstract states 'consistent gains' in content quality, embodied teaching quality, assessment, and personalization, but cites no specific quantitative metrics.
The paper was posted June 2026 on arXiv (cs.CL) and surfaced on Hugging Face Daily Papers with 35 points; no code repository or demo link is referenced in the abstract.
summary: LectūraAgents is an arXiv preprint (June 2026) proposing a multi-agent framework for personalized AI-assisted learning built around a hierarchical orchestration design. A ProfessorAgent leads a team of specialized subordinate agents through research, planning, review, and lecture delivery, with the distinguishing feature being "embodied teaching": the system executes parameterized visual actions (handwriting, highlighting, underlining) over a virtual teaching environment rather than simply generating static content. The core algorithmic contribution is the Teaching Action-Speech Alignment (TASA) algorithm, which applies salience-based heuristics and temporal semantic segmentation to align those actions with the spoken lecture track and a per-learner profile. Evaluation covers high school through graduate courses via rubric-based analysis validated by expert educators; the authors claim consistent gains over prior approaches across content quality, teaching action quality, assessment, and personalization, but the abstract provides no quantitative figures. The paper's practical interest for agent practitioners is the hierarchical orchestration design and the TASA temporal-alignment mechanism; the educational domain framing is secondary to those structural contributions.
novelty: embodied teaching mechanism with adaptive action sequences
audience: educators and AI researchers in personalized learning

title: LoopCoder-v2: Only Loop Once for Efficient Test-Time Computation Scaling
why: For engineers thinking about test-time compute scaling, this paper establishes that looping more is not monotonically better: a carefully characterized gain-cost trade-off saturates at exactly two loops, and knowing why matters for any architecture that reuses weights across depth.
notes: Parallel Loop Transformers (PLT) avoid sequential looping's latency and KV-cache scaling penalties by using cross-loop position offsets (CLP) and shared-KV gated sliding-window attention, making loop count a tunable hyperparameter rather than a pure cost.
LoopCoder-v2 is a 7B PLT family trained from scratch on 18T tokens, with separate variants at different loop counts; all variants receive matched instruction tuning before evaluation.

The two-loop variant improves SWE-bench Verified from 43.0 to 64.4 points and Multi-SWE from 14.0 to 31.0 points over the non-looped baseline across code generation, reasoning, agentic SE, and tool-use benchmarks.

Variants with three or more loops regress, not merely plateau; diagnostics show loop 2 provides the main productive representational refinement, while later loops produce oscillatory updates and reduced representational diversity.

The paper frames this as a gain-cost trade-off: CLP introduces a fixed positional mismatch at each loop boundary; as refinement gains shrink past loop 2, the offset cost increasingly dominates, providing a principled explanation for the non-monotonic saturation.
summary: LoopCoder-v2 is a 7B-parameter Parallel Loop Transformer (PLT) family trained from scratch on 18T tokens to study loop-count selection for test-time computation scaling. PLT avoids the latency and KV-cache costs of sequential looping via cross-loop position offsets (CLP) and shared-KV gated sliding-window attention. Empirically, the two-loop variant yields large gains over the non-looped baseline, raising SWE-bench Verified from 43.0 to 64.4 and Multi-SWE from 14.0 to 31.0, while three-or-more-loop variants regress. The authors attribute saturation to a gain-cost trade-off: each additional loop incurs a fixed CLP-induced positional mismatch, and as the representational refinement signal decays, that offset cost dominates. Diagnostic analysis confirms that loop 2 is the primary source of useful representational change, with subsequent loops producing diminishing and oscillatory updates, providing a mechanistic basis for selecting loop count in PLT architectures.
novelty: non-monotonic loop-count effect in Parallel Loop Transformers
audience: ML researchers and practitioners working on efficient test-time computation scaling

title: ClinHallu: A Benchmark for Diagnosing Stage-Wise Hallucinations in Medical MLLM Reasoning
why: Knowing that a medical MLLM hallucinated because it misread the image versus misapplied clinical knowledge versus made a flawed inference requires different fixes; ClinHallu is the first benchmark structured to make that causal attribution tractable.
notes: 7,031 validated instances, each annotated with a structured reasoning trace decomposed into three discrete stages: Visual Recognition, Knowledge Recall, and Reasoning Integration.

Stage-replacement interventions are the core diagnostic mechanism: the benchmark measures how correcting a specific stage's output causally affects the final answer, enabling per-stage error attribution rather than aggregate hallucination rates.
The authors find hallucination sources vary per sample; no single stage dominates, which motivates instance-level rather than model-level diagnosis.

Trace-supervised fine-tuning, training on the structured reasoning traces, is reported to reduce stage-wise hallucinations, but the abstract provides no quantitative improvement figures or confidence intervals.

The paper does not name which base MLLMs were evaluated, and the benchmark's coverage of imaging modalities (radiology, pathology, etc.) is not described in the abstract, leaving scope and generalizability unclear from available text.
summary: ClinHallu is a publicly released benchmark of 7,031 validated instances designed to diagnose the origin of hallucinations within medical multimodal large language model reasoning chains, rather than merely counting them. Each instance is annotated with a structured reasoning trace decomposed into three stages: Visual Recognition, Knowledge Recall, and Reasoning Integration. The key methodological contribution is stage-replacement intervention: by substituting correct outputs at a specific stage and observing the effect on the final answer, the benchmark attributes errors causally to particular reasoning steps. The authors argue that prior medical hallucination benchmarks focus on data collection and final-output evaluation while ignoring where failures are introduced in the reasoning pipeline. Beyond evaluation, the paper reports that fine-tuning on the structured traces (trace-supervised fine-tuning) reduces stage-wise hallucinations, though no quantitative figures are provided in the abstract. Which base models were tested and what imaging modalities are covered are not specified in the available text.
novelty: Stage-wise hallucination diagnosis in medical multimodal large language models
audience: ML researchers working on trustworthy medical models

title: Show HN: Kage – Shadow any website to a single binary for offline viewing
why: kage addresses the structural fragility of JS-dependent web archiving by rendering pages with real headless Chrome before stripping all scripts, producing portable, network-free artifacts (ZIM archives, self-contained executables, native desktop apps) that are designed to remain readable indefinitely.
notes: Uses a pool of headless Chrome tabs to render each page to its fully settled DOM, then strips all `<script>` tags, inline event handlers, and `javascript:` URLs; CSS, images, and fonts are fetched separately and link-rewritten to deterministic local paths before the corresponding page is even finished downloading.

Packs mirrors into the open ZIM format used by the Kiwix ecosystem (offline Wikipedia, Stack Overflow, Project Gutenberg); kage-produced ZIM files are structurally valid and browsable in any ZIM reader, but lack the full-text search index that official Kiwix packs ship with.

`--format binary` appends the ZIM archive to a kage executable producing a single self-contained viewer with roughly 13 MiB of fixed overhead; cross-platform viewers are built by pointing `--base` at any platform's kage release binary, with the target inferred from the executable header.
Packing is deterministic: the same mirror content always produces a byte-identical archive with a content-derived UUID rather than a randomized one, making it safe to checksum, diff, and cache.

The default build is pure Go (CGO_ENABLED=0); building with `-tags webview` links WKWebView (macOS), WebView2 (Windows), or WebKitGTK (Linux) to open a native window instead of the system browser, but this remains opt-in because it requires CGO and complicates cross-compilation.
summary: kage is a Go CLI tool that archives websites for offline reading by driving headless Chrome to render each page to its settled DOM state, then stripping all JavaScript and rewriting asset links to local paths via a deterministic URL-to-path mapping. The crawler is breadth-first, idempotent, and resumable on interrupt; it respects robots.txt and seeds from sitemap.xml by default, with flags for scoping by path prefix, subdomain inclusion, lazy-load scrolling, and concurrency. Mirrors can be previewed with `kage serve`, packed into a ZIM archive (the open standard behind Kiwix), or compiled into a self-contained executable that serves the site with no external dependencies. Cross-platform self-contained viewers are produced by swapping the `--base` binary to another platform's kage release; the base overhead is approximately 13 MiB regardless of site size. Desktop app packaging is supported: a macOS .app bundle, a Linux AppImage, and a Windows GUI-subsystem .exe (console-free) are all available output forms. One stated limitation: kage-produced ZIM archives omit the full-text search index that official Kiwix packs include, so in-reader search does not work, though browsing and link navigation do.
novelty: deterministic website cloning for offline viewing
audience: developers and security researchers

title: $\mu_0$: A Scalable 3D Interaction-Trace World Model
why: A frozen, action-label-free world model that matches the manipulation performance of supervised VLA systems like π₀ would substantially reduce the data-collection bottleneck that currently constrains cross-embodiment robot learning.
notes: μ₀ occupies a middle ground between pixel-space video prediction (wastes capacity on appearance) and direct action modeling (requires embodiment-specific labels): it forecasts smooth 3D trajectories only for salient interaction points — objects, tools, hands, and contact regions — representing each as B-spline control points.

TraceExtract is the automated supervision pipeline: it selects keypoints from raw video, constructs globally aligned 3D traces, and associates motion segments with hierarchical language captions, enabling training from diverse unannotated video sources without robot action labels.

The architecture couples a pretrained vision-language backbone with a modular trace expert; μ₀ is trained once and then kept frozen, so downstream robot embodiments attach only a thin action expert on top of the fixed trace interface.

The paper claims trace-conditioned policies — trained without action supervision — achieve performance competitive with π₀, a VLA pretrained with explicit action labels; the comparison is presented as the primary evaluation result, though the paper is an arXiv preprint and independent replication has not yet occurred.

μ₀ is evaluated on both 2D and 3D trace prediction benchmarks and reportedly outperforms trace-prediction baselines and tokenized VLM methods, but benchmark details and ablations are in the full PDF and not summarized in the abstract.
summary: μ₀ is a world model that sidesteps the embodiment-specificity problem in robot learning by predicting compact 3D motion traces for interaction-relevant keypoints rather than reconstructing dense pixels or predicting raw actions. The representation — smooth trajectories over objects, tools, hands, and contact regions, parameterized as B-spline control points — is designed to be embodiment-agnostic and reusable across robot morphologies. Training data comes from the authors' TraceExtract system, which automatically lifts keypoint trajectories from heterogeneous video into globally aligned 3D supervision paired with hierarchical language captions, removing the need for robot-specific action annotation. At inference time, μ₀'s weights are frozen; a small, embodiment-specific action expert is trained on top of its trace outputs, decoupling world-model pretraining from policy fine-tuning. The central empirical claim is that these trace-conditioned policies match the manipulation performance of π₀, a VLA model pretrained with full action supervision, establishing 3D traces as a viable and scalable intermediate representation for cross-embodiment learning; the paper is a June 2026 arXiv preprint and results await independent replication.
novelty: scalable 3D interaction-trace world model for robot learning
audience: robotics engineers and researchers

title: Apple Foundation Models
why: Apple's OS 27 unifies on-device and server-side models behind a single `LanguageModelSession` API, and this official Anthropic package makes Claude a protocol-conforming drop-in, so iOS/macOS developers can escalate to frontier reasoning or server-side tools by changing one argument rather than rewriting request logic.
notes: The package targets Apple's Foundation Models *server-side* language model API, introduced in OS 27 betas (macOS 27); requests travel app-to-Claude API directly, Apple is explicitly not in the request path and does not see prompts or responses.

Switching between on-device (`SystemLanguageModel`) and Claude requires only swapping the `model:` argument on `LanguageModelSession`; respond(to:), streaming, guided generation, and tool calling all use the same call sites.

Each compiled-in `ClaudeModel` constant encodes its own capability set (sampling params, effort levels, adaptive thinking, structured output, image input); the package uses this to decide which request fields to send, and treats sending an unsupported field as a hard error rather than silent degradation.

`fixedEffort:` exposes five levels (low/medium/high/xhigh/max) including `xhigh` and `max`, which the framework's own reasoning-hint interface does not reach; the field is validated against the chosen model's declared capability set.

Server-side tools (web search with per-domain allow/blocklists and `maxUses`, web fetch, code execution) run on Anthropic infrastructure and must be configured on `ClaudeLanguageModel` rather than `LanguageModelSession`; different tool sets require separate model instances.

Beta caveats: v0.1.0, APIs may change before GA, external pull requests are not being accepted, and a macOS 27 host is required to run the included example; production auth should use `.proxied` to avoid bundling an extractable API key in the binary.
summary: Anthropic has published `ClaudeForFoundationModels` (Apache 2.0, v0.1.0, beta), a Swift package that conforms Claude to Apple's `LanguageModel` protocol as part of the server-side language model API introduced in OS 27 betas. Developers address both Apple's on-device model and Claude through the identical `LanguageModelSession` interface, switching between them by changing the `model:` argument; requests go directly from the app to the Claude API, with Apple absent from the request path. The package surfaces streaming via cumulative snapshots, `@Generable`-driven structured output (throwing `unsupportedGenerationGuide` rather than silently degrading on incapable models), client-side tool calling, and server-side tools including web search with domain filtering and use-count limits, web fetch, and code execution. A per-constant capability system on `ClaudeModel` governs which request fields are sent; `fixedEffort:` additionally unlocks `xhigh` and `max` effort levels that the framework's own reasoning-hint interface does not expose. The package is not a general-purpose Messages API client, its public surface is intentionally narrow, and external pull requests are not being accepted during the beta period.
novelty: Integration of Claude with Apple's Foundation Models framework
audience: Developers using Apple's Foundation Models framework

title: Building an serial and VGA "everything console"
why: A concrete, sub-$150 recipe for a self-contained portable serial-plus-VGA console that can cold-connect to headless servers, POWER9 iron, or 1980s minicomputers without depending on network access or a working OS.
notes: The base unit is a surplus IBM 7316-TF3 rack KVM (2004-2014 vintage): 17" LCD, 1280x1024 max, optimal at 60Hz; the author notes 75Hz produced more artifacts.

The VT100 terminal brain is a Tattler Solutions box ($86 shipped, USB-powered, DE-9 serial, up to 115200bps); it ships from the US and has documented, hardware-level inability to handle USB hub/combo devices, which ruled out the IBM SK-8845RC UltraNav keyboard.

Two manual USB and VGA switchboxes ($20 total) give the unit dual modes: VT100 serial terminal, or raw USB+VGA passthrough to an external host, with the IBM keyboard and panel shared between both paths.
Demonstrated as a serial console for an AT&T 3B2/310 and as a VGA console for a POWER9 Raptor Blackbird (via VGA-to-HDMI dongle on the Blackbird side).

Practical fabrication caveat: silane metal adhesive between metal surfaces requires roughly a week to fully cure, not the 24 hours stated on the tube; the author's first attempt failed at the 24-hour mark and had to be redone.
summary: The author acquires a beat-up IBM 7316-TF3 1U rack KVM (17\" panel, USB keyboard with TrackPoint/trackpad) and rebuilds it into a self-contained, portable console supporting both VT100 serial and raw VGA/USB passthrough modes. The terminal logic comes from a Tattler Solutions VT100 box ($86 shipped): USB-powered, 115200bps DE-9 serial, with solid VT100 support but a hard hardware constraint preventing use with USB hub or combo devices, which forced swapping the IBM UltraNav for a $20 Perixx slimline keyboard. Two manual USB and VGA switchboxes route the shared panel and keyboard to either the terminal box or a pair of external connectors, effectively reproducing the original KVM's passthrough function alongside the new serial capability. A mini power strip with USB ports ($13) and Velcro-based cable management consolidate power internally. Mechanical work included Z-bracket shims and multi-layer adhesive to correct a keyboard-height problem that prevented the unit from closing, with the lesson that silane adhesive between metal needs a week to cure. The finished unit is shown running as a serial console for an AT&T 3B2/310 and as a VGA console for a POWER9 Raptor Blackbird via a VGA-to-HDMI adapter on the server side.
novelty: custom-built serial and VGA console
audience: embedded systems engineers

title: ‘Tell Him He’s a Piece of Shit’: Meta’s New AI Unit Is a Total Mess
why: Meta is conscripting roughly 6,500 senior engineers into synthetic data-labeling work with no exit option short of resignation, exposing how frontier AI labs are scaling training pipelines by converting product engineering headcount into human-in-the-loop annotation labor.
notes: Meta's Applied AI (AAI) unit, formed March 2026 to support Meta Superintelligence Labs, redirected ~6,500 engineers and product managers to generating synthetic coding puzzles and software problems used to train and evaluate frontier models; some members report completing as few as two tasks per week.

Participation is mandatory: engineers are given no option other than joining AAI or leaving the company, prompting employees to describe themselves as 'draftees'; manager-to-report ratios on some AAI teams deliberately reached 50:1.

The restructuring accompanied a 10% company-wide reduction (8,000 employees, framed as AI-focused), and three anonymous current employees describe morale as record-low, characterizing the work as 'soul-crushing,' 'mechanical,' and a sharp downgrade from prior product engineering roles.

Separately, 1,600-plus Meta employees signed a petition against a program that monitors employee clicks and keystrokes to generate AI training data; Meta's concession is a 30-minute opt-out pause and case-by-case exemptions, not a program halt.

Zuckerberg's internal memo, seen by WIRED, framed AAI as a temporary waypoint while 'other roles' are created, vowed no further mass layoffs in 2026, and announced plans to cap manager ratios, increase event budgets, and restore assigned desks by year-end; Meta declined to comment on the story, so employee characterizations of working conditions are unverified by the company.
summary: Meta's Applied AI (AAI) unit, assembled in waves since early April 2026 under a March reorganization, has concentrated roughly 6,500 engineers and product managers into a team whose primary output is synthetic coding problems and evaluation puzzles for frontier model training. Membership is non-optional: engineers must join or leave, a practice unusual enough in Silicon Valley that employees have taken to calling themselves draftees. Three anonymous current employees told WIRED the work is menial and underutilizes their skills, with deliberate management ratios as high as 50:1 on some teams; Meta's CPO Chris Cox separately acknowledged a "brutal" environment at an all-hands meeting, and Zuckerberg's own internal memo conceded the company has "made mistakes." Alongside AAI discontent, more than 1,600 employees signed a petition against a Meta program that harvests employee click and keystroke data for AI training, to which management responded with limited opt-out provisions rather than cancellation. The article is sourced from three anonymous current employees, leaked internal recordings, and an internal Zuckerberg memo; Meta declined to comment, leaving employee characterizations of morale and working conditions without official rebuttal.
novelty: Internal dissent at Meta's Applied AI team
audience: Senior software engineers interested in AI and industry dynamics

title: SoK: The Constant Time Model
why: Confirmed timing side-channels in private key loading in both OpenSSL and BoringSSL—with BoringSSL leaking orders of magnitude harder despite its stricter threat model—expose a systemic gap between constant-time theory and deployed TLS stacks that matters right now.
notes: SoK format: systematizes divergent definitions of 'constant time' across academia and industry, showing that the concept is not uniformly agreed upon and that models have evolved inconsistently.
Core structural finding: constant-time models protect cryptographic primitives, but specifications often assume broader protection—creating an exploitable boundary that existing models do not cover.

Authors develop an offensive methodology targeting this specification/primitive boundary and apply it to find a timing vulnerability in private key loading, confirmed in production code in both OpenSSL and BoringSSL.

Counterintuitive empirical result: BoringSSL's per-observation timing signal is 'several orders of magnitude stronger' than OpenSSL's, despite BoringSSL having an explicitly stricter constant-time threat model—the stricter policy appears to create unexpected amplification.
Caveat: the abstract is spare on mechanism details; readers will need the full PDF for specifics on attack methodology, measurement setup, and signal magnitudes.
summary: This SoK paper systematizes how "constant time" has been defined and applied across academic and industry cryptographic contexts, exposing significant divergence in assumptions and scope. The authors identify a recurring structural gap: constant-time models are designed to protect primitive-level operations, yet higher-level specifications implicitly assume much broader coverage. Leveraging this insight, they develop an offensive methodology that hunts for timing vulnerabilities at the specification/primitive boundary rather than within the primitive itself. Applying it, they confirm a specification-level timing leak in private key loading in both OpenSSL and BoringSSL. The most operationally striking result is that BoringSSL—which adopts an explicitly stricter constant-time threat model than OpenSSL—produces a per-observation timing signal several orders of magnitude larger, illustrating how stricter formal policies can paradoxically amplify leakage when the specification layer is not covered by the model.
novelty: Three distinct contributions: (1) a unified taxonomy of constant-time definitions across the literature; (2) an offensive methodology that shifts the attack surface from primitive internals to the specification/integration boundary—largely unaddressed by prior work; (3) empirical confirmation that BoringSSL's stricter threat model amplifies, rather than suppresses, timing signal in private key loading.
audience: Cryptography engineers and security researchers who audit or maintain TLS/crypto library code.

title: Anthropic cuts off Fable 5 and Mythos 5 access following government order
why: A US government export control order just forced Anthropic to globally cut off its own frontier models—including for its own foreign-national employees—while Anthropic publicly disputes the evidentiary basis, setting a live precedent for state control over AI model access.
notes: Government ordered blocking of Fable 5 and Mythos 5 for all foreign nationals—including Anthropic's own non-US staff—with no written disclosure of the specific national security threat; evidence was communicated verbally only.

Anthropic's public statement asserts no disclosed jailbreak produced a harmful result and none are Mythos-specific; the company cites GPT 5.5 as having equivalent vulnerabilities, arguing no unique 'uplift' exists from its models.

To satisfy the foreign-national blocking requirement, Anthropic cut off access for all customers globally rather than attempt selective per-user enforcement—suggesting technical and legal complexity in targeted gating.
Company states it had pre-emptively worked with US and UK governments and updated its data retention policy to track misuse attempts before the order arrived, framing itself as a cooperative actor.

The 'no specific details provided' framing echoes historical tensions in national security letters and FISA orders; Anthropic's willingness to publish a rebuttal is notable given typical gag-order constraints in such directives.
summary: The US government issued an export control directive requiring Anthropic to block access to its frontier models Fable 5 and Mythos 5 for all foreign nationals, including its own employees, citing national security concerns it declined to specify in writing. Anthropic complied but issued a public statement contesting the order's evidentiary foundation: disclosed jailbreak vulnerabilities were characterized as minor, were communicated only verbally, produced no demonstrated harmful output, and are available via competing models such as GPT 5.5—implying no model-specific uplift. To satisfy the foreign-national gating requirement, Anthropic cut off access entirely for all customers globally rather than attempt selective enforcement. The company notes it had taken proactive steps before the directive, including collaborating with US and UK governments and revising data retention policies. This represents one of the most direct and sweeping government interventions in frontier AI model access to date, and Anthropic's public dispute over the evidence standard may shape how AI companies respond to future such orders.
novelty: First documented instance of a government export control order blocking AI model access for a company's own foreign-national employees; Anthropic's public challenge to the evidentiary standard—verbal-only disclosure, no harmful result demonstrated, no model-specific uplift—is a novel corporate response posture that could establish precedent for industry pushback on AI-targeted national security orders.
audience: AI policy watchers, developers dependent on Anthropic APIs, and security researchers tracking export control precedents in the AI stack.

title: Anthropic to disable its most advanced AI models after US order limiting foreign access
why: The US government has, for the first time, applied export controls directly to AI model access rather than chips—disabling Anthropic's flagship Fable 5 and Mythos 5 for all customers globally and setting a precedent that could reshape how frontier AI is deployed and regulated worldwide.
notes: First-of-its-kind export control directive targets AI model access itself (not chips or training infrastructure), issued by the Commerce Department against Fable 5 and Mythos 5.

Government's stated rationale: a jailbreak bypassing cybersecurity guardrails on Fable 5 that could enable software vulnerability discovery; Anthropic says it received only 'verbal evidence of a potential narrow, non-universal jailbreak'—no technical specifics shared.
To comply with the foreign-national restriction, Anthropic must disable both models for ALL customers globally, not just foreign nationals—a blunt technical constraint they call disproportionate.

Backdrop: Anthropic is already on a US supply chain blacklist after refusing military use for domestic surveillance and autonomous weapons; the company also filed confidentially for IPO last month, giving this maximum commercial and political significance.

Experts quoted say Mythos-class models could 'dramatically accelerate sophisticated cyber-attacks' on legacy banking infrastructure, but Anthropic notes rival providers show 'similar ability to unearth minor bugs in code'—suggesting selective enforcement risk.
summary: The US Commerce Department issued an export control directive ordering Anthropic to suspend access to its newest frontier models, Fable 5 and Mythos 5, for all foreign nationals, citing national security concerns over an alleged jailbreak enabling offensive cybersecurity use. Lacking the ability to surgically restrict foreign nationals at the model layer, Anthropic announced it will disable both models globally for all customers. The company contests the action, arguing it received only verbal, non-specific evidence of a narrow jailbreak and that rival models show comparable code-vulnerability capabilities. This marks a significant policy escalation: previous US AI export controls focused on semiconductor supply chains, not direct restrictions on deployed commercial AI services. The move lands as Anthropic is simultaneously navigating a supply chain blacklist (for refusing autonomous-weapons and domestic-surveillance military use) and a pending IPO filing, putting it in direct conflict with the Trump administration on multiple fronts. The Pentagon CIO publicly backed the directive, framing it as "America First" over revenue.
novelty: First application of US export controls to restrict access to a deployed commercial AI model directly—not chips, not training data, not weights, but live API/product access. Also notable: the government's enforcement mechanism requires global model takedown rather than targeted foreign-national blocking, exposing a gap between policy intent and technical feasibility.
audience: AI engineers, security researchers, and policy-watchers at or building on frontier model APIs who need to understand how national security directives can instantly yank access to production-grade models.

title: TLA+ mental models
why: A practitioner who has shipped TLA+ on production AWS systems (Aurora DSQL, CosmosDB) finally makes explicit the tacit modeling judgment that separates TLA+ users from TLA+ thinkers — and that judgment, not syntax, is now the actual bottleneck.
notes: Demirbas draws from 8 industry projects including CosmosDB consistency modeling (5 consistency levels as predicates over operation histories) and Aurora DSQL secondary index; MongoDB distributed transactions spec written post-production as authoritative description

The 'Slow is Fast' paper (cited, authored by Demirbas) formalizes when stale-state guards are safe: a guard is locally stable if only the node's own actions can falsify it — such guards require less coordination and tolerate delays, enabling more concurrency
Ernie Cohen and the StableEmptySet project are cited as examples of pushing atomic actions to finest granularity to avoid distributed locking; TLC handled the resulting interleaving explosion

LLM claim is asserted but unsupported: 'people are getting value by generating TLA+ models and counterexamples directly from Google Docs descriptions' — no benchmarks, methodology, or examples provided; treat as anecdote
CALM (Consistency As Logical Monotonicity, UC Berkeley) named as making monotonicity concrete; Lamport's Paxos derivation used as the canonical thread connecting all 7 models
summary: Murat Demirbas, who has led TLA+ adoption on production AWS systems including Aurora DSQL and CosmosDB, articulates seven mental models for effective TLA+ use derived from industry practice.
